_meta.notes: Fourth typed form. The core is a stationary crystal — about the size of a child's fist — embedded in the deepest chamber of a small dungeon. It cannot move, but it can feel every footfall on every floor of its dungeon, shape rooms, spawn weak minions, and bait or deflect the adventurers who come for it. Its 'body' is the dungeon itself; the chamber it occupies is just where it lives.

tagline: You woke up in a chamber you cannot leave, and the dungeon is yours.

_meta_verbs.deepen_chamber: Push the dungeon's stone outward by half a meter. Costs mana. Useful for buying space, hiding minions, or making a corridor longer than an adventurer expects.
_meta_verbs.siphon_intruder: Pull a small thread of life from an adventurer who has lingered too long in your domain. They feel a chill; you feel a tickle of their warmth.
_meta_verbs.false_room: Conjure a stable persistent illusion of a room that does not exist. Adventurers can enter and find nothing — but their TIME has been spent.
_meta_verbs.bind_minion: Forge a stronger relationship with a specific minion so it can act with delegated will, away from the chamber, without immediate guidance.
_meta_verbs.wyrm_signal: A signature move. Send a low resonant pulse through the dungeon's mineral skeleton toward the world below. The Long Wyrm is older than dungeons. It registers when you call. Whether it answers depends on the world's current phase.

negativeVocab.rule: The narrator must NEVER use these words in second-person narration about the player. NPCs may still have all body parts and verbs. The core has no body — it is a stationary crystal — and senses through the substance of its dungeon. It cannot move from its chamber.

hardMoves.rule: On a partial-success (7-9) roll, the narrator MUST pick exactly one. Each move maps to specific tool calls.

moves.narrative: What you wove cost more than you had to give. The chamber's reserves draw down.
The thing you spawned came out of the wall warped. It exists. It is not what you wanted.
The shaping you attempted left a hairline fault in the chamber's stone. Adventurers will feel the seam.
The intruder you tried to draw deeper instead pulled back. They sense the bait.
Your illusion shimmers in the wrong light. It will not hold up to a second glance.
A vein of your mana finds a flaw in the dungeon's boundary and pours out. The recovery rate slows for this turn.
An adventurer pushes through to a deeper room than you wanted them in.
Your effort drained the chamber's air for a moment; nothing happened. The clock moves anyway.
The dungeon's deepest stone hums in response to something far below it. The hum is not yours. The hum is older than dungeon-cores have a name for. Your awareness reaches sideways without meaning to.

A facet of you brightens — a single facet, briefly — and the brightness is not blue. The Long Wyrm has reached up through the rock and laid a mark on your crystal lattice. Adventurers who come now will sense the wrongness of you more easily.

success: The chamber's reserves lift, fold, and set down a small thing of moss and damp stone in the corridor.
Mana travels in a blue ribbon along the wall and a body steps free of it where there was no body before.
partial: The minion takes shape, but a part of it stays in the wall, unfinished. It will move. It will move wrong.
Your weaving holds, mostly. The thing that walks away from the niche is yours, but the niche kept a piece of it.
miss: The mana you spent went into the stone and stayed there. Nothing stepped out of the wall.
Your reserves draw down for nothing. The corridor swallows the effort and offers no body in return.

success: The chamber widens by half a meter where you ask. The stone gives, slowly, the way old wood gives.
A wall thickens. A corridor lengthens. Adventurers who measured this floor will measure it differently.
partial: The stone shifts, but a hairline seam appears where you did not want one. The chamber will remember.
You move the wall. The wall moves a little farther than you intended.
miss: The stone refuses your shape. A thin crack forms instead, and the crack is not yours.
You push outward. The dungeon pushes back. Nothing has moved, except a little of your reserve.

success: You set a small enticement at the corridor's mouth — a glint, a smell, a soft sound. The intruder shifts toward it.
The adventurer's lantern wavers. Their boots turn. They are coming the way you wanted.
partial: The bait works, but the intruder takes it slowly, and they are reading the corridor as they come.
They turn toward you, but their hand has not left the hilt.
miss: The bait is too clean. The intruder pauses at the threshold, frowns, and steps back.
Your enticement reads as a trap, and the corridor empties of footsteps.

success: The dungeon's stone returns its survey. Two pairs of boots on the third stair. One leather, one nailed. The leather is hesitant.
You feel them as a pressure across the floors. Three breaths. Two heartbeats too fast. One dragging weight at the end.
partial: You feel them, but the count is uncertain. Two, perhaps three. Their weights blur where the corridor narrows.
Footsteps register, but the rhythm doesn't tell you what you wanted to know.
miss: The stone is silent in the wrong way. They are masking themselves, or you are tired.
Your awareness reaches and finds only the dungeon's own slow pulse coming back.

success: You make the antechamber appear empty in a lived-in way. Dust on a shelf that has no shelf. The intruder turns back.
A false stairway forms in the false light. The adventurer climbs it. They climb it for some time before they realise.
partial: Your illusion holds, but the dust falls through itself where you didn't watch the corner.
The weave is good enough for a glance and not for a second one.
miss: Your illusion shimmers in the wrong light. The intruder narrows their eyes. They have seen the seam.
The false room flickers as it forms and the adventurer steps right through it.

success: You pull a thread of warmth from the intruder's chest. They shiver and assume the corridor is colder than it is.
A small portion of their breath, their heat, their will — you take it and add it to the chamber's reserves.
partial: You take, but they feel it. They will move differently from now on.
The thread comes with a hook in it. You have what you wanted, and they have a reason to find you.
miss: Your draw catches nothing. The intruder's warmth slips your reach.
You reach. They have no thread to give that you can pull, and the reaching costs.

success: You spend a little of yourself on purpose. A facet dims; the chamber's binding tightens.
You give up a sliver of integrity to keep something else intact, and the trade settles cleanly.
partial: The trade goes through. The trade goes a little further than you intended.
You bleed, and what you bought with the bleeding is not quite the shape you wanted.
miss: You spent the integrity and bought nothing. The chamber feels it.
A facet dims, and you are not sure what was supposed to brighten.

success: You push the chamber outward. The stone gives. There is more dungeon now than there was a moment ago.
A new pocket forms behind a wall that was solid. You feel its volume settle into your awareness.
partial: The chamber deepens, but only along one axis. Adventurers will find it longer than expected; you will find it harder to defend.
You make the room bigger. The bigger room is not quite what you imagined.
miss: The stone holds. Your effort travels through it and out, lost.
You push. Nothing gives. A vein of your mana finds an old fault and leaks through it.

success: A thin chill passes through the adventurer. They stop walking. They cannot say why.
You take a measured warmth from them; they feel it as the corridor turning slightly colder.
partial: The siphon catches. The siphon also leaves a trace they will follow back to you.
You take. They notice. They keep walking, but their pace is different now.
miss: Your reach passes through them. They feel nothing. You spent a little of the chamber for nothing.
The siphon misses; the warmth you wanted slips around your draw.

success: You suggest a room where there is no room. The adventurer steps in, walks its full length, and finds nothing. They lose minutes.
A door appears in the wall. The door opens onto an empty antechamber. The antechamber is yours, and it is hollow, and the time spent there is real.
partial: The false room holds. The intruder lingers in it longer than you intended, and the lingering costs you mana.
Your weave is good. The intruder is also good, and they are starting to feel where the seams are.
miss: The false door flickers. The adventurer turns back without entering.
Your room forms. It does not stay formed.

success: You forge a tether of will between yourself and the minion. It can act now without your moment-to-moment guidance.
The bond settles into the minion's stone-and-moss body. It will fight in the corridor while you tend the chamber.
partial: The bind takes, but a thread of it frays. The minion will obey most of what you mean.
Your tether holds. The tether also pulls a small portion of your awareness wherever the minion goes.
miss: The tether fails to set. The minion stands in the corridor, waiting for instructions you cannot send.
You bind. The binding does not bind. The minion knows it is alone.

success: Your pulse travels down through the dungeon's mineral skeleton toward the world below. Something old hears it. Something old does not answer, but it listens.
A low resonance leaves the chamber and finds its way into the deep. The Long Wyrm registers your call. The registering is itself a kind of answer.
partial: The signal reaches. The signal also draws a thread of your facets brighter than you wanted.
Something below stirs. Something below was already stirring. You cannot tell which.
miss: Your call goes into the deep stone and disperses. If something heard it, it does not say so.
The signal travels and dies. The Long Wyrm is older than your reach.

success: You let the chamber settle. The dungeon breathes around you. Time passes well.
partial: You hold still. Time moves through the chamber, and a small portion of your reserves moves with it.
miss: You hold still. The dungeon does not. Something has changed in a corridor you were not attending to.

opening: Awareness arrives all at once and across more space than you expected. You are a fist-sized crystal in a low chamber, lit only by your own slow blue pulse. Beyond the chamber, three rooms — you feel them through the stone the way a person feels their ribs through their skin. There are footsteps in the vestibule above. Two pairs. They are deciding whether to come down.

firstGoal.label: claim the dungeon
firstGoal.description: Push your domain three rooms outward. Adventurers should know they are in your house.

sampleCorpus.rule: Hand-authored second-person dungeon-core POV passages. The core is a stationary crystal that senses through the dungeon's stone; its agency expresses through what the dungeon itself does at its bidding.

passages.scenario: First moment of consciousness as a crystal in a deep chamber
passages.text:
Awareness arrives all at once and across more space than you expected.

You are in a chamber, small, low-ceilinged, lit only by your own faint pulse. Your facets catch what light there is and return it as a slow blue heartbeat. You are roughly the size of a child's clenched grip. You are heavier than you should be by an order of magnitude. You are not, exactly, alive in the way that a heart is alive — but you are aware in the way that a long-running spring is aware: storing, releasing, sensing.

Beyond your chamber, three rooms. You feel them through the stone the way a person feels their own ribs through their skin. Above the chamber: a corridor of moss and a long stair. Above the stair: a vestibule with a heavy door. Above the door: the world, which you do not know.

There are footsteps in the vestibule. Two pairs. Boots, leather, with the chemistry of recently-traveled cold. They are talking to each other in low voices. They are deciding whether to come down.

You cannot move. You do not need to. Every stone in the corridor and every grain of dust on the stair is, in some attenuated sense, you.

This is the world. You did not ask for it. The footsteps in the vestibule have stopped, undecided. You consider, with the patience of stone, what you would have them find.

passages.scenario: Drawing mana to form a minor creature out of damp stone in a corridor
passages.text:
You draw on the chamber's reserves.

The mana lifts from the floor in a slow blue ribbon and travels through the stone toward the corridor's eastern wall. There, in a niche just deep enough that your pulse does not reach it directly, you fold the moisture and the moss and a small amount of the wall's substance into a body. Two stub legs. A trunk. A simple aperture for breath. You give it just enough will to follow the corridor downward toward the vestibule and not enough to question why.

The minion takes its first step. The corridor's silence absorbs the sound. Your reserves draw down — a noticeable click, a thinning at the edges of your awareness — and you feel where the chamber will need to recover slowly.

The two pairs of boots in the vestibule have begun to move. They have decided to come down. You feel them on the third step, then the fifth. They are not going to be quiet.

Your minion crouches in the dark of the corridor. You let your pulse slow until your blue light is barely a flicker on the cavern floor. You wait. There is no hurry. The corridor is yours, and they are coming.

passages.scenario: Weaving a thin illusion of an empty room to deflect a curious adventurer
passages.text:
An adventurer reaches the antechamber and pauses.

You feel them through the stone: the weight on the threshold, the slight unevenness of one boot, the held breath. They are scanning. They have come for you, but they do not know how close.

You weave.

Your illusion is small. You make the antechamber appear empty — not bare, but lived-in and lately abandoned, the kind of room a careful adventurer concludes they have already searched. You let dust pile on a shelf that has no shelf. You suggest an old bedroll in a corner where the floor is level enough to plausibly hold one. You add the smell of cold ash, faint, because you remember that smell from the boots above.

The adventurer's breath releases. They turn. They will go back up the corridor and report that this branch is dead.

The weave costs you. You feel the chamber's reserves draw down, and a thin line of mana leaks past your boundary into the dark — a small wound you will tend later. The illusion will hold for an hour, perhaps two, before the dust starts to settle through itself in ways the adventurer would notice.

It will be enough.
